sources.verification_method: Founder narrative: David Cramer's personal blog 'Sentry: From the Beginning' documents the project's history. Origin of the open-source code is 2008 (Cramer's unlicensed Django plugin), but Functional Software, Inc. — the legal entity currently operating Sentry — was incorporated separately. Per METHODOLOGY 7.1, founded_year tracks the legal entity, not the project. Cra.mr is the founder's personal blog (source.type='other' because it does not fit company-website / tech-press / press-release enums; it is a primary first-person founder source which is the strongest available narrative for company-founding history).

CIO piece (Jan 2020) on Sentry as a bootstrapped unicorn references '8 years since incorporation as of January 2020', implying incorporation around 2012. Combined with the Cramer narrative (Heroku AddOn launch end-of-2012, 'David founded Sentry in January 2012'), founded_year=2012 is the best available triangulated estimate. Per METHODOLOGY 3.2, ≥2 independent sources for the high-stakes claim. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. Exact Delaware Division of Corporations incorporation certificate for Functional Software, Inc. was NOT retrieved during Phase 1 (Atlas Free Preview egress allowlist does not include sec.gov / Delaware corporate registry endpoints). TODO post-Free-Preview: validate against Delaware certificate of incorporation; if exact incorporation date differs from 2012, update with HIGH confidence.

Sentry's current LICENSE.md (HEAD master, browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09) declares 'Copyright 2008-2024 Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry'. The 2008 start of the copyright claim reflects the original year of the open-source code (assigned to the entity at incorporation), NOT the year the entity itself was incorporated. Atlas uses 2012 per METHODOLOGY 7.1 ('Year of legal incorporation of the jurisdictional entity that currently operates the company'). Pablo's ruling on 2026-05-09 explicitly chose 2012 over 2008 to maintain consistency with MongoDB (founded_year=2007 = 10gen Inc. incorporation) and CockroachDB (founded_year=2015 = Cockroach Labs Inc. incorporation), both of which apply 7.1 strictly to legal entities, not to project origins. METHODOLOGY 7.1 NOT amended — this case is canonical 7.1 application, not an edge requiring rule extension.

Crunchbase Sentry profile lists headquarters as San Francisco, California, United States. ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code: US. Cross-confirmed by sentry.io official press release (Series E, May 2022) which describes Sentry as 'San Francisco-based market leader in developer-first application monitoring'. Source.type='other' used because Crunchbase is a private-market data provider not currently in the schema enum (analog to Tracxn/PitchBook/Forge Global handling in earlier records; see schema TODO section 8 for proposed v1.1 'fundraising-database' enum).
Sentry official Series E press release (sentry.io, May 2022) describes the company as 'San Francisco-based'. Independent corroboration of Crunchbase per METHODOLOGY 3.2.

Sentry official Series E press release describes the company as 'San Francisco-based'. Atlas convention per METHODOLOGY 7.2: canonical short form 'San Francisco' (no 'San Francisco, CA' redundancy at this field — the U.S. state qualifier belongs in narrative context, not the canonical city short form). Cross-confirmed by Crunchbase, Sacra (sacra.com/c/sentry/), Tracxn 2026.

Series E (USD 90M, May 2022) is the most recent disclosed funding round. The schema enum 'series-e-plus' is the catch-all for Series E and later. No Series F or later round announced through 2026-05-09 (verified against sentry.io press releases page, blog.sentry.io, TechCrunch, PRNewswire, PitchBook 2026 profile, Crunchbase, Tracxn 2026).

PitchBook 2026 Sentry company profile reports aggregate total raised of USD 217M across all disclosed funding rounds. Manual sum of disclosed rounds: Seed (1.5M, 2015) + Series A (9M, 2016) + Series B (16.5M, 2018) + Series C (40M, 2019) + Series D (60M, 2021) + Series E (90M, 2022) = USD 217.0M EXACTLY. Per METHODOLOGY 7.4 private-company convention. Unlike CockroachDB (where Series A-F manual sum left an implied USD 6.4M undisclosed seed gap to reconcile to the 633M aggregate), Sentry's manual sum reconciles exactly with the aggregate — no implied undisclosed rounds. CONFIDENCE: HIGH.

Sacra private-market research profile for Sentry independently reports USD 217M total raised, consistent with PitchBook 2026 aggregate. Cross-corroborated by Crunchbase organization page and Tracxn 2026 ('Sentry has raised a total of $217M over 6 funding rounds'). Four independent database sources converge on the same figure. Per METHODOLOGY 3.2, ≥2 independent sources for high-stakes claim. NOTE: source.type='other' used because Sacra is a private-market data provider not currently in the schema enum (consistent with prior Tracxn/Forge Global handling).

Sentry official Series E press release (May 2022) announces USD 90M raised at 'more than $3 billion' post-money valuation. Co-led by BOND and Accel, with NEA and K5 Global participating. This is the company's most recent disclosed funding round; no subsequent round announced through 2026-05-09. Per METHODOLOGY 7.5 private-company convention, last_valuation_usd captures the post-money declared in the most recent disclosed round. Atlas value: USD 3,000,000,000 (the press release language 'more than $3 billion' is rounded to the nearest billion as the published headline figure; precise post-money was reported as approximately USD 3B by tech press and private-market DBs).

The SaaS News piece on Sentry Series E independently reports USD 3B post-money valuation. Cross-confirmation per METHODOLOGY 3.2. Additional corroboration: Sacra (sacra.com/c/sentry/) and PitchBook 2026 both list Series E post-money at USD 3B. CAVEAT: snapshot is ~4 years old at data_collection_date (2026-05-09); SaaS valuation reset 2022-2023 may have impacted real fair value, but no public down-round, secondary tender, or revaluation event has been reported for Sentry in primary sources. The 3B figure should be interpreted as 'most recent disclosed post-money', not 'current fair value' (analog to CockroachDB last_valuation_usd handling).

Sacra private-market research confirms Sentry's private status as of 2026-05-09. No SEC filings exist for Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry (no S-1, no 10-K, no Form D filings discoverable through SEC EDGAR direct search by entity name). Cross-confirmed by Crunchbase organization page (status: private) and Tracxn 2026 (latest disclosed liquidity event = Series E, no IPO announcement). NOTE: source.type='other' used per Sacra/Crunchbase pattern.

license_history.rationale_public: Per Sentry's 2019-11-06 'Re-Licensing Sentry' announcement (David Cramer): the relicense was driven by the same free-rider problem that Cockroach Labs cited five months earlier (June 2019) — cloud providers and competing observability vendors offering Sentry-as-a-service or rebadged Sentry monitoring without contributing back. BSL-1.1 with Sentry's chosen 36-month conversion to Apache-2.0 forbids commercial competing offerings during the conversion window while remaining free for end-user / internal-deployment use; converted code is automatically Apache-2.0 after the conversion period.

Per Sentry's 2023-11-20 'Introducing the Functional Source License' announcement (David Cramer): the BSL Additional Use Grant mechanism had developed inconsistent semantic interpretations across implementers (MariaDB, Redis, HashiCorp each defining 'competing use' differently), creating user confusion and legal uncertainty. Sentry developed FSL as a successor to BSL with three explicit changes: (a) shorter conversion delay (24 months vs 36 months on Sentry's prior BSL configuration); (b) a fixed 'Permitted Purpose' clause replacing the negotiable Additional Use Grant; (c) per-version conversion semantics with explicit SPDX-style license identifier (FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 or FSL-1.1-MIT). FSL is published openly at fsl.software for adoption by other companies; Liquibase has subsequently adopted FSL.

sources.verification_method: Live LICENSE.md file at master HEAD of getsentry/sentry, browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09 (Chrome screenshot). The file's title is 'Functional Source License, Version 1.1, Apache 2.0 Future License' and the explicit 'Abbreviation:' field declares 'FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0'. Notice section: 'Copyright 2008-2024 Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry'. File is 105 lines (68 LOC), 3.7 KB. Atlas canonical short form per METHODOLOGY 7.18 = 'FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0' (SPDX-canonical, not abbreviated to bare 'FSL' — variants exist: FSL-1.0-Apache-2.0, FSL-1.1-MIT, FSL-1.0-MIT, and the variant matters). controlled-vocabularies.yaml updated in commit c820708 (2026-05-09) to fix prior drift (was 'FSL-1.1-Apache' without -2.0; now 'FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0' SPDX-canonical).

Sentry official 2023-11-20 announcement explicitly names the new license 'FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0' (the variant being adopted). Independent corroboration of the LICENSE.md content per METHODOLOGY 3.2 (≥2 independent sources for high-stakes claim). Additional triangulation: TechCrunch 2023-11-20 (Frederic Lardinois, contemporaneous with announcement), The Register 2023-11-20, InfoQ 2023-12, fsl.software (Sentry's own FSL spec hosting page).

Sentry retrospective on its license history explicitly states: 'Sentry started life in 2008 as an unlicensed, 71-line Django plugin. The next year [2009] we began publishing it under BSD-3, and ten years later [2019] we switched to the Business Source License.' Confirms BSD-3-Clause as the first published license (2009-2019). Per METHODOLOGY 7.7 hierarchy item (1): direct official statement from the company. Atlas convention per Pablo ruling 2026-05-09: license_history captures 2 entries (BSD-3-Clause → BSL-1.1 and BSL-1.1 → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0); the 2008 unlicensed period (~12 months) is documented in metadata.notes but NOT included as a license_history entry because 'unlicensed' is not in the schema license vocabulary and adds noise without analytical value. SPDX-canonical short form: 'BSD-3-Clause' (not bare 'BSD-3' — schema vocabulary uses BSD-3-Clause per controlled-vocabularies.yaml line 139).

Source for the 2019 BSD-3-Clause → BSL-1.1 license_history entry. Commit ca8674c ('meta(LICENSE): Switch to Business Source License 1.1 (#15470)') is the canonical artifact: it physically replaced the LICENSE file in getsentry/sentry to BSL-1.1. The commit message references the public announcement at blog.sentry.io/2019/11/06/relicensing-sentry/ and is dated 2019-11-06 — same day as the public announcement (Atlas captures year=2019, month=11). Per METHODOLOGY 7.8: commit URL is the strongest primary source for license-change events.

Source for the rationale_public of the 2019 BSD-3-Clause → BSL-1.1 transition. Sentry's official 'Re-Licensing Sentry' blog post (David Cramer, 2019-11-06) explains the move was inspired by Cockroach Labs' June 2019 BSL adoption. Per METHODOLOGY 7.7 hierarchy item (1): direct official statement. The 36-month BSL conversion delay was Sentry's chosen configuration of BSL-1.1 (BSL allows licensees to set their own change-date).

Source for the 2023 BSL-1.1 → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 license_history entry. Sentry's 2023-11-20 announcement is the canonical primary source per METHODOLOGY 7.7 hierarchy item (1). NOTE: the exact GitHub commit that physically replaced LICENSE.md in getsentry/sentry from BSL-1.1 to FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 was NOT extracted during this data collection — github.com commits-history HTML pages render dynamically and web_fetch returns boilerplate without commit listings; api.github.com (which would resolve the blame view deterministically) is outside the cowork-egress allowlist. Per METHODOLOGY 7.8, commit URL is the 'strongest primary source' but explicitly acknowledged as ideal-not-required when the company blog is the canonical announcement (analog to CockroachDB 2019 BSL transition where the same constraint applied — see CockroachDB metadata.notes item 10). TODO post-Free-Preview: extract via local clone (`git -C /path/to/sentry log --follow --oneline LICENSE.md | head -50`) and add as additional github-license-file entry.

Independent corroboration of the 2023-11-20 BSL-1.1 → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 transition. TechCrunch piece (Frederic Lardinois) reports the announcement contemporaneous with the company blog post, including: (a) the new license name (FSL); (b) the 24-month conversion period; (c) the choice of Apache-2.0 as the change license; (d) Sentry's stated rationale of avoiding the inconsistent BSL Additional Use Grant interpretations across MariaDB / Redis / HashiCorp. Per METHODOLOGY 3.2: ≥2 independent sources for high-stakes claim. Additional corroboration: The Register 2023-11-20, InfoQ 2023-12, fsl.software.

Current LICENSE.md is a single FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 covering all main Sentry web app code; no clause offers an alternative concurrent license that the user can elect. The FSL time-delayed conversion to Apache-2.0 (effective 24 months after each version is made available) does NOT make this dual licensing per METHODOLOGY 7.9 strict definition ('same source code offered under two concurrent licenses, with the user choosing which one applies'). Per METHODOLOGY 7.18 (added 2026-05-09 in commit c820708): 'time-delayed automatic conversion does not count as dual_license=true unless the same current source code is simultaneously offered under user-selectable alternative licenses' — this is the canonical Atlas application of 7.18. The conversion mechanism (per-version, 24 months, post-conversion = Apache-2.0) is documented in metadata.notes.

Sentry's CONTRIBUTING.md (browser-fetched 2026-05-09 via web_fetch) is intentionally minimal — 11 lines, 8 LOC, 379 bytes, two sections: 'Feedback, Advice, and General Help' (directing to GitHub Discussions) and 'Contributing to Sentry' (linking to docs.sentry.io/internal/ for server development documentation). NO mention of Contributor License Agreement (CLA), Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO), sign-off requirement, or out-of-band contributor agreement page. Per METHODOLOGY 7.10 hierarchy: item (1) out-of-band company legal page = NOT found (sentry.io/legal/ surfaces DPA but no contributor agreement); item (2) visible CLA bot integration on PRs = NOT confirmed (cla-assistant.io/getsentry/* endpoint not surfaced via WebSearch; no CLA Assistant or EasyCLA bot integration found in repo workflows surfaced via search); item (3) CONTRIBUTING.md statement = explicit absence; item (4) DCO sign-off = not mentioned. Conclusion: cla_required=false. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. The absence of evidence at hierarchy items 1, 3, 4 plus silence at item 2 is consistent with cla_required=false but is not as strong as positive confirmation via a sample PR review showing no bot intervention. TODO post-Free-Preview: Pablo browser-validate a recent PR in getsentry/sentry to confirm absence of CLA-bot intervention; also check develop.sentry.dev internal docs (which CONTRIBUTING.md links to) for any contributor-agreement language. Methodologically distinct from MongoDB (out-of-band CLA via legal page) and CockroachDB (CLA Assistant bot integration) — Sentry takes a third position consistent with its public anti-CLA posture (FSL design rationale explicitly cites avoiding contributor-permanent-assignment via time-delayed Apache-2.0 conversion).

Sacra private-market research profile for Sentry identifies multiple parallel commercial offerings as of 2024-2025: (1) Sentry Cloud SaaS at sentry.io with free tier + paid self-serve tiers (Team starts at USD 26/mo, Business, Enterprise); (2) Self-hosted Sentry under FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 (free for non-Competing-Use deployments, no revenue threshold gating — distinct from CockroachDB CSL which has a USD 10M revenue cap on the free self-hosted tier); (3) Enterprise self-hosted contracts with named enterprise customers (GitHub, Disney, Atlassian, Reddit, Slack referenced by Sacra; Microsoft, Dropbox, Disney by Contrary Research). Three+ material parallel offerings places Sentry into 'hybrid' per METHODOLOGY 2.1 — and 2.1 explicitly lists Sentry as a canonical example of the 'hybrid' enum. EDGE CASE NOTE per METHODOLOGY 2.1 strict definition: 'hybrid' assumes the existence of an OSS layer ('OSS + hosted + enterprise tiers'). Post-2023-11-20, Sentry has NO OSS layer in the OSI sense — FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 is source-available proprietary with the time-delayed Apache-2.0 conversion. The OSS layer technically does emerge per-version after the 24-month conversion delay (versions older than 24 months ARE Apache-2.0), but the HEAD/latest GA release that anyone forking today encounters is FSL — that is the canonical license per METHODOLOGY 7.18. Sentry classified 'hybrid' in Atlas v1 as a least-bad fit, identical resolution to CockroachDB post-CSL (see CockroachDB metadata.notes item 14). The case reinforces the schema TODO from METHODOLOGY section 8 for a v1.1 enum value 'source-available-tiered' to capture the FSL/CSL/SSPL/Elastic-2.0 pattern more precisely without forcing the 'hybrid' fit.

Contrary Research Sentry Business Breakdown independently confirms the 'hybrid open-source and self-serve SaaS business model' classification, citing the same multi-offering structure: (a) free open-source SDKs (which are MIT/Apache permissive, NOT FSL — the SDKs are the developer-facing gateway product); (b) free FSL self-hosted Sentry server; (c) Cloud SaaS with free + paid tiers; (d) enterprise contracts. NOTE on multi-repo license heterogeneity: while the main getsentry/sentry repo is FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0, the SDKs (sentry-python, sentry-javascript, sentry-java, sentry-cocoa, sentry-ruby, sentry-laravel, sentry-react-native, sentry-dotnet, etc.) are typically Apache-2.0 or MIT permissive — this is the cara pública del producto for developers and is intentional product strategy: the SDKs are designed to embed in user code, so they need to be permissive for adoption, while the server-side aggregation/storage is gated commercially. This pattern was flagged during Phase 2 pre-research and is documented here in commercial_model.type rather than license fields because 'license heterogeneity across product surfaces' is fundamentally a commercial-model design choice, not a license-history fact about the canonical primary_repo.

Sacra private-market research explicitly states 'self-serve SaaS model accounts for 70% of Sentry's revenue', with the remaining ~30% from enterprise contracts and other sources. Self-serve Cloud SaaS is the dominant revenue channel; this is the primary_revenue_stream. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM (single private-market research source for the exact 70% split; Sentry has not publicly disclosed its revenue mix as a private company). Schema enum 'cloud' applied per the dominant 70% Cloud SaaS revenue. Cross-record observation: MongoDB primary_revenue_stream='cloud' (Atlas SaaS dominant); CockroachDB primary_revenue_stream='enterprise-license' (self-hosted majority); Sentry primary_revenue_stream='cloud' — Sentry matches MongoDB pattern (cloud-dominant), distinct from CockroachDB pattern (self-hosted-dominant). This is methodologically meaningful for Hypothesis 4 (or whichever hypothesis tests revenue-mix patterns by category): databases skew enterprise-license, observability skews cloud — early N=3 signal worth tracking.

Contrary Research independently corroborates the Cloud-dominant revenue thesis: 'Sentry employs a hybrid open-source and self-serve SaaS business model. The self-serve model accounts for 70% of Sentry's revenue' (matching Sacra's figure exactly — likely both citing the same upstream Sentry investor-deck data point or each other; this is not 2 fully independent confirmations of the 70% figure but rather 2 sources reporting the same number from a shared origin). Sacra additionally reports unverified ARR estimate of USD 128M at end of 2023 with 30% YoY growth — this is the order-of-magnitude commercial scale context, NOT a high-confidence ARR claim (Sentry has not publicly disclosed ARR; both Sacra and Contrary mark ARR figures as estimates).

metadata.notes: Phase 1-3 complete as of 2026-05-09 (data collection date). Phases 4-7 pending. KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 1: (1) founded_year=2012 per METHODOLOGY 7.1 strict (year of legal incorporation of the jurisdictional entity that currently operates the company = Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry). Sentry originated in 2008 as David Cramer's unlicensed Django plugin / open-source project, and the current LICENSE.md (browser-validated by Pablo 2026-05-09) declares 'Copyright 2008-2024 Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry'. The 2008 start of the copyright claim reflects the original year of the open-source code, assigned to the entity at incorporation; NOT the year the entity itself was incorporated. Atlas uses 2012 because the field tracks the legal entity, not the codebase origin. Triangulated from: (a) cra.mr/sentry-from-the-beginning/ (David Cramer founder narrative — Heroku AddOn launch end-of-2012, 'David founded Sentry in January 2012'); (b) cio.com Jan 2020 piece referencing '8 years since incorporation as of January 2020' (2020 - 8 = 2012). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. Exact Delaware Division of Corporations incorporation certificate for Functional Software, Inc. was NOT retrieved during Phase 1 (Atlas Free Preview egress allowlist does not include sec.gov / Delaware corporate registry endpoints; Crunchbase pages also outside cowork-egress). TODO post-Free-Preview: validate founded_year=2012 against the Delaware certificate of incorporation; if exact incorporation date differs (e.g., 2011 if entity was registered before product launch, or different month within 2012), update with HIGH confidence. METHODOLOGY 7.1 NOT amended — this case is canonical 7.1 application, not an edge requiring rule extension. Pablo ruling on 2026-05-09: 2012 over 2008 to maintain Atlas consistency with MongoDB (founded_year=2007 = 10gen Inc. incorporation) and CockroachDB (founded_year=2015 = Cockroach Labs Inc. incorporation), both of which apply 7.1 strictly to legal entities, not to project origins. (2) hq_city='San Francisco' per METHODOLOGY 7.2 canonical short form (no 'San Francisco, CA' redundancy). Cross-confirmed against Crunchbase, Sacra, Tracxn, sentry.io official Series E press release. (3) total_raised_usd=USD 217M derived per METHODOLOGY 7.4 (private-company variant). Manual sum of disclosed rounds: Seed (1.5M, 2015) + A (9M, 2016) + B (16.5M, 2018) + C (40M, 2019) + D (60M, 2021) + E (90M, 2022) = 217M EXACTLY. No implied undisclosed seed/pre-A round (contrast with CockroachDB which had a 6.4M reconciliation gap). Four independent DBs (PitchBook 2026, Sacra, Crunchbase, Tracxn 2026) converge on 217M. CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (4) last_valuation_usd=USD 3B reflects post-money declared in Series E (May 2022). The press release language 'more than $3 billion' is rounded to the nearest billion as the published headline figure; precise post-money was reported as approximately USD 3B by tech press and private-market DBs. CONFIDENCE: HIGH on the headline figure with documented imprecision (the 'more than' qualifier means the exact post-money may have been slightly above 3B, e.g., 3.05B-3.2B; private-company convention captures the rounded headline). Caveat: snapshot is ~4 years old at data_collection_date; SaaS valuation reset 2022-2023 may have impacted real fair value, but no public down-round, secondary tender, or revaluation event has been reported. Interpret as 'most recent disclosed post-money', not 'current fair value' (analog to CockroachDB handling). (5) Source.type='other' used for Crunchbase and Sacra (private-market data providers) consistent with prior Tracxn/Forge Global handling in MongoDB and CockroachDB records. Schema TODO section 8 captures the proposed v1.1 enum addition 'fundraising-database' / 'private-market-data'. (6) FSL-related decisions deferred to Phase 2. Pre-research completed 2026-05-09 with all six A-F decisions validated by Pablo before opening this file (timeline: 2008 unlicensed → 2009 BSD-3-Clause → 2019-11-06 BSL-1.1 → 2023-11-20 FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0). METHODOLOGY 7.18 added as standalone hygiene commit (commit c820708) before Phase 1 began, codifying the time-delayed conversion rule for FSL/BSL families. controlled-vocabularies.yaml updated in the same commit to fix a vocabulary drift (FSL-1.1-Apache → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0). KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 2: (7) primary_current='FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0' per METHODOLOGY 7.18, browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09 against the literal LICENSE.md 'Abbreviation:' field at github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/master/LICENSE.md. Each Sentry release independently converts to Apache-2.0 on its second anniversary (per-version conversion, 24-month delay, post-conversion = Apache-2.0). HEAD/latest GA at data_collection_date is FSL — that is the canonical license per 7.18. A user who deliberately checks out a release tag from >24 months ago is using a converted Apache-2.0 version, but per 7.18 that does not redefine primary_current. (8) primary_first='BSD-3-Clause' (SPDX-canonical short form, schema vocabulary at controlled-vocabularies.yaml line 139). The 2008 unlicensed period (~12 months from project origin to 2009 BSD-3-Clause adoption) is documented here in metadata.notes but NOT included as a license_history entry per Pablo ruling 2026-05-09: 'unlicensed' is not in the schema license vocabulary and adds noise without analytical value. license_history therefore captures 2 entries (BSD-3-Clause → BSL-1.1 in 2019-11; BSL-1.1 → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 in 2023-11), matching the entity-level legal-license history. (9) The 2019-11-06 BSL-1.1 transition source uses commit ca8674c ('meta(LICENSE): Switch to Business Source License 1.1 (#15470)') as the canonical commit per METHODOLOGY 7.8. The official blog announcement (blog.sentry.io/relicensing-sentry/) is referenced for rationale_public. Rationale: same week / same day as the GitHub commit; David Cramer's blog post explicitly cites Cockroach Labs' June 2019 BSL adoption as inspiration. Sentry's chosen BSL configuration was 36-month conversion to Apache-2.0 (BSL allows licensees to set their own change-date). (10) The 2023-11-20 FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 transition source uses the official Cockroach-Labs-style fallback per METHODOLOGY 7.7 hierarchy item (1): blog.sentry.io/introducing-the-functional-source-license-freedom-without-free-riding/ as the canonical announcement, with TechCrunch / The Register / InfoQ as independent corroboration per 3.2. The exact GitHub commit that physically replaced LICENSE.md was NOT extracted during this data collection: github.com commits-history HTML pages render dynamically and web_fetch returned boilerplate without commit listings; api.github.com is outside the cowork-egress allowlist; `gh` CLI is not installed in the sandbox. Per METHODOLOGY 7.8, commit URL is the 'strongest primary source' but explicitly acknowledged as ideal-not-required when the company blog is canonical (same constraint and resolution as CockroachDB 2019 BSL commit — see CockroachDB metadata.notes item 10). TODO post-Free-Preview: extract 2023 FSL commit hash via local clone of getsentry/sentry (`git log --follow --oneline LICENSE.md | head -50`) and add as additional github-license-file source entry. (11) dual_license=false per METHODOLOGY 7.18 explicit application: time-delayed automatic conversion (FSL per-version 24-month conversion to Apache-2.0) does NOT count as dual licensing because it is NOT user-selectable concurrent. The conversion happens automatically as a function of release age; the user does not choose between FSL and Apache at any given moment. This is the canonical Atlas application of 7.18 to its first qualifying record. (12) cla_required=false with MEDIUM CONFIDENCE based on absence-of-evidence across METHODOLOGY 7.10 hierarchy: (a) out-of-band company legal page — NOT found at sentry.io/legal/ (only DPA); (b) visible CLA bot — NO cla-assistant.io/getsentry/* endpoint surfaced; no CLA Assistant or EasyCLA bot in repo workflows; (c) CONTRIBUTING.md (browser-fetched 2026-05-09 via web_fetch on github.com) — only 11 lines / 8 LOC / 379 bytes, two sections (Feedback / Contributing), NO mention of CLA, DCO, sign-off, or contributor agreement; (d) DCO — NOT mentioned. Conclusion: cla_required=false is the most consistent reading. Methodologically distinct from MongoDB (out-of-band CLA via legal page = HIGH confidence true) and CockroachDB (CLA Assistant bot integration = HIGH confidence true): Sentry takes a third position. This is consistent with Sentry's public anti-CLA posture — the FSL design rationale explicitly cites avoiding the contributor-permanent-assignment problem of traditional CLAs by relying on time-delayed Apache-2.0 conversion to release contributor work. The MEDIUM confidence (vs HIGH) reflects the absence-of-evidence nature of the conclusion: a sample PR review by Pablo on a recent getsentry/sentry pull request would confirm the absence of CLA-bot intervention and upgrade to HIGH. TODO post-Free-Preview: Pablo browser-validate one or two recent PRs in getsentry/sentry to confirm no CLA bot triggers; also check develop.sentry.dev internal docs (linked from CONTRIBUTING.md) for any contributor-agreement language not surfaced in the public CONTRIBUTING.md. (13) CROSS-RECORD OBSERVATION (cla_required pattern emerging across 3 verified records): MongoDB has out-of-band CLA via legal page; CockroachDB has CLA Assistant bot; Sentry has neither — three distinct positions across three records. This is a methodologically rich early signal: cla_required is not converging to a single 'open-core company default' pattern. If subsequent records continue to show heterogeneity, the field will be analytically valuable for Hypothesis 5 (or whichever hypothesis tests contributor-friction structures). If subsequent records cluster into 1-2 dominant patterns and Sentry remains an outlier, it becomes a falsification signal worth examining. KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 3: (14) commercial_model.type='hybrid' is the consensus fit across two converging arguments: (a) METHODOLOGY 2.1 explicitly lists Sentry as a canonical example of 'hybrid' (the methodology was authored before Sentry's 2023 FSL transition, but the multi-offering structure has not changed); (b) Sacra and Contrary Research both classify Sentry's business as 'hybrid open-source and self-serve SaaS'. EDGE CASE: post-2023-11-20 FSL transition, Sentry has NO OSS layer in the strict OSI sense at HEAD/latest GA release — same structural problem encountered with CockroachDB post-CSL (which forced 'hybrid' as least-bad fit; documented in CockroachDB metadata.notes item 14). The OSS layer DOES emerge per-version after 24 months via FSL's auto-conversion, but per METHODOLOGY 7.18 primary_current measures HEAD which is FSL. Sentry classified 'hybrid' as least-bad fit, reinforcing the schema TODO (METHODOLOGY section 8) for a v1.1 'source-available-tiered' enum value to capture the FSL/CSL/SSPL/Elastic-2.0 pattern. NOTE on multi-repo license heterogeneity: getsentry/sentry main repo is FSL, but Sentry SDKs (sentry-python, sentry-javascript, sentry-java, sentry-cocoa, sentry-ruby, sentry-laravel, sentry-react-native, sentry-dotnet, etc.) are Apache-2.0 or MIT permissive — intentional product strategy because SDKs embed in user code and need permissive licenses for adoption. The Atlas captures the canonical primary_repo (getsentry/sentry, FSL) per precedent (MongoDB main = mongodb/mongo, CockroachDB main = cockroachdb/cockroach), but the SDKs being permissive is a meaningful commercial-strategy fact and is documented at the commercial_model.sources verification_method level rather than as a license_history fact. (15) commercial_model.primary_revenue_stream='cloud' per Sacra's 70% / 30% split (self-serve Cloud SaaS dominant, enterprise + other = 30%). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. Single private-market research source (Sacra) for the exact percentage — Contrary Research reports the same 70% but it is likely citing the same upstream data. Sentry has NOT publicly disclosed its revenue mix; the 70%/30% figure is a third-party analyst estimate, not company-disclosed. Sacra additionally cites unverified ARR estimate of USD 128M at end of 2023 with 30% YoY growth as scale context, NOT a high-confidence claim (both Sacra and Contrary mark ARR figures as estimates). (16) CROSS-RECORD OBSERVATION (primary_revenue_stream pattern emerging at N=3): MongoDB='cloud' (Atlas SaaS dominant); CockroachDB='enterprise-license' (self-hosted Enterprise majority); Sentry='cloud' (70% Cloud SaaS). Sentry matches MongoDB pattern, distinct from CockroachDB pattern. The N=3 signal is consistent with a category-level hypothesis: databases (which compete with cloud-provider managed services) skew toward self-hosted Enterprise revenue because cloud-provider competition makes self-hosted licensing the differentiated channel; observability and developer-tools (which compete with API-based SaaS like Datadog/New Relic) skew toward Cloud SaaS revenue because the SaaS form factor IS the product. This early hypothesis is testable as more records are verified — if Grafana (observability) shows cloud-dominant primary_revenue_stream, signal strengthens; if Camunda or BPM tools show self-hosted-dominant, the category hypothesis fragments and individual product-market-fit dynamics dominate. Methodologically meaningful early signal worth tracking. (17) Sentry's revenue model design — 'free for non-Competing-Use' under FSL with NO USD revenue threshold — is materially distinct from CockroachDB's CSL Free (USD 10M revenue cap). This means: a USD 50M-revenue startup running self-hosted Sentry with no commercial competing offering pays $0 to Sentry (compliant with FSL Permitted Purpose), while a USD 50M-revenue company running self-hosted CockroachDB pays the per-CPU CSL Enterprise rate. The functional difference: CockroachDB monetizes on customer scale (revenue threshold); Sentry monetizes on customer use case (Cloud SaaS convenience + enterprise SLAs). This mechanical difference explains why CockroachDB primary_revenue_stream='enterprise-license' (the License Key issuance is the ALL-self-hosted-customer channel post-2024) while Sentry primary_revenue_stream='cloud' (self-hosted is largely free, paid customers self-select into Cloud).
